data.title: Vision Investing With Cirrus Logic
data.description:
Perhaps investors have developed multiple approaches to generating successful monetary returns. In our view, there are two, one being vision, understanding and relying on an accurately researched vision of future potential. This approach generally ignores short-term financial model reports. Reports such as these offer little long-term insight. Models, with significantly less value, might include short-term or long-term growth histories, valuation, discounted cash flows (DCF), earnings or other financial analyses. Instead of monitoring financials, the prudent investor research's market potential, financial and/or product barriers (competitors), capital security (does it have access to enough cash to survive the early years), synergy, compelling products, product development timing, and probably the most important, human capital or better stated, development execution. Relating to the last, both product development and production become paramount. In our view, vision investments must have both development and production excellence because without one, the approaching financial model collapses.

In this article, our choice of example is Cirrus Logic, Inc. (CRUS), an investment we have owned for more than three decades. This continues our coverage of the company, the last being Cirrus Logic's Moderately Valued Price Suggests Patience. In the article, we argued for patience due to our belief that lower stock prices were likely. Prices in the low to mid-hundred teens did come and go. We would have bought shares in that middle range.

Yet, it appears that the eye-doc's tools stated above are helpful in spotting long-term vision investments like Cirrus Logic. Let's go find out how good!

Long-term Vision: A Valuable Investment Style

We religiously followed, and still do, Peter Lynch's principle-based advice: invest in what you know, look for contrarian value and don't prune the buds. Of particular note, this is primarily principle and potential-based rather than heavily weighted in finances. His success was unprecedented.

A Vision Reviewed

Our Cirrus interest came from an article appearing late in a 1994 Money Magazine discussing the extremely low valuation of the stock less than a tenth of its recent high, Peter Lynch's second principle. The company in 95 primarily produced state-of-the-art graphic accelerators for PCs. Yet, a few years earlier, Cirrus purchased an Austin-based start-up, Crystal Logic, with its patent-protected state-of-the-art mixed-signal products, chips with both an analog and digital component. The role of this technology provides an interface between the analog and digital worlds. This is an extremely difficult endeavor, with digital producing troublesome noise cascading into the analog function. Most applications, in particular sound or voice can't tolerate noisy analog processes, which is Crystal's targeted market. Our interest in the up-and-down progress with Cirrus became its mixed-signal potential, a mirror of our profession. This illustrates our adherence to Lynch's first principle: invest in what you know. We dealt with this daily at work. The extreme difficulty in developing this technology provides a natural entrance barrier. Today, Cirrus is almost completely alone having purchased the only real audio competitor, England-based Wolfson, several years ago.

Although this sound market remained small through the 90s, a truly transformative period opened early in the second millennium, when Steve Jobs returned to Apple Inc. (AAPL). His vision of targeting mobile markets with transformative products, first the iPod followed by iPhone, iPad, iWatch and others, opened huge doors for Cirrus' technology. In 2007, the company won, from Wolfson, the codec with its ability to internally integrate a capacitance function. Cirrus shortly afterward developed and won the amplifier market. With mobile, optimizing three critical attributes became paramount: size, power usage (lowest being best) and noiseless performance. Cirrus developed the best-in-class optimization. This remains so.

Continuing to discuss the perfect match, Apple's planned demand perfectly matched execution to coincide with its plans. A new management team set out to create the then almost non-existent human capital for both product and manufacturing. Cirrus successfully created a best-in-class employee work environment in the early period. We watched intently and closely for it was with this that the immense market size coming with Apple would materialize. This remains a critical attribute.

We noted above the critical value of understanding, valuing, and trusting in market potential. How is it sized? How long will the market last? Can the products be improved, or will natural attrition eventually replace them? With Apple Computer's entrance into the mobile markets and shortening its name in 2007 to Apple, enormous potential approached though exact amounts remained fuzzy.

In addition, product life plus product development cycles add critical investment timing for holding and buying shares of stock. Product development cycles may create an illusion of no growth with massive growth lurking in the near future. Product longevity or lack thereof significantly impacts investment strategies.

Value in Synergy

One additional mostly undervalued element, synergy, adds depth and security to the investment. In the early days, Cirrus had a high-margin product market with A/D D/A interfacing for industrial, power monitoring, seismic testing, professional and others. The technology developed for this market in essence paid the bills for developing audio and sound. With audio more secure in place, the company is now developing the next extension, power functions, which are still undetermined for both sides of the battery. We will discuss more details later. Management also extended the reach of the audio into laptop markets. The strange entrance was a direct result of competitors in this market no longer being able to provide parts adequately during 2020 and 2021. The customers soon experienced the Cirrus product superiority and are now designing with custom PC parts.

Defining Vision vs. Execution Investments

We noted two types, the second is execution based, the transition between the two is primarily defined in our view, which is the dog and which is the tail. It is about potential. Although definitions might be personal, this difference matters. Knowing which parameters remain paramount helps investors decide on an investment approach, buy/sell, then reload or sell short calls for periods of time that present themselves as enriching opportunities. Understanding this difference also helps investors pay attention to proper metrics. Finances mostly take a second seat, if any seat at all. It is mostly about potential and faith that this potential is at least somewhat real.

With companies that have reached a level of execution, obviously, paying attention to established business performance is still very important. For Cirrus, the last quarterly report beat significantly analysts' expectations. From a Seeking Alpha headline, Cirrus Logic Non-GAAP EPS of $2.83 beats by $0.45, revenue of $561M beats by $19.59M, bullish reports continued illustrating the strong health of the business performance for the execution portion. For the year, the business is on target for between $8 - $8.5, creating a reasonable value for stock prices in the $110 - $130 range.

Still A Vision Investment

In our view, Cirrus remains a vision investment with lots of somewhat fuzzy growth opportunities coming, even strong possibilities for revenue to more than double. Before we discuss in more detail future growth, John Forsyth, CEO, during the last call, reminded investors of this:

We consider this longevity a significant strength of our business as it provides solid long-term visibility and sustained revenue contribution, along with the ability to leverage our R&D resources in new areas that can drive further innovation and growth.

Forsyth just told us that new business profits fall directly to the bottom line, a change from the past that has massive implications for earnings growth.

So, it's time to review the future with a level of added detail. Cirrus' future growth falls within three categories:

  • Control products on both sides of a battery.
  • PC audio and haptics.
  • High margin products such as industrial, pro-audio, auto, etc.

Beginning with the first, management now focuses resources, some once dedicated to new codec products, on the battery. A slide from the call could help investors more accurately understand the long-term path.

The graphic shows the several-year path of winning sockets primarily with Apple. Not in the slide, Cirrus lost a haptic socket worth approximately $1 a few years back because the companion part from another vendor failed. At that event, management noted its disappointment but included commentary that this loss over the long term was almost immaterial. The comment offers a window into the size of coming ASPs.

Answering a question from Tore Svanberg, of Stifel, on the battery-targeted products, Forsyth added,

And I think we've seen some validation of that from the comments reflected back to us from customers when we've been sharing silicon with them and details on what we've been developing. We've got a number of irons in the fire there.

The CEO also made clear that final products or design wins aren't yet.

In our view, the battery products include at least:

  • Power distribution.
    • ASPs $1 - $2 per phone.
    • Based on Lion Semiconductor technology (Capacitor).
    • Extremely efficient with simpler and smaller sizes.
  • Charging conversion.
    • Again, $1 - $2 ASP.
    • Based on Lion Semiconductor technology.

Although this product projection makes sense and is our best guess, we could be wrong. But, with the statement made by management concerning the $1 product, it seems to support the content ASP gains in the range predicted above.

Also, it seems important to note the importance of the recent fab partnership with Global Foundries, targeting U.S. fab locations with products of a very different structure. What, if any, role will this new technology add?

Should Cirrus win iPhone sockets, the major target, a $3 ASP increase adds $6 per share of profit at 250 million per year iPhone units sold. This is an approximate $100 change in stock value. These state-of-the-art products could easily displace older less efficient competitive technology within mid-to-flagship models outside of Apple. This would approximately double the potential market. A win with Apple could drive the stock price steeply higher.

Next, the PC audio, haptic and charging products are discussed. Again, a slide can help.

Cirrus Logic Presentation

The targeted SAM continues to increase, now in the $1.2B range.

A progress summary follows:

  • Both "consumer and commercial laptops featuring our components to come to market over the next year as the adoption of SoundWire device class audio accelerates," expands our footprint.
  • Cirrus released a new codec that delivers enhanced voice activation even in low-power modes.
  • Won the first mainstream-priced laptop.

The new features are part of the expanding SAM. With continued measured penetration, stock price effects are likely measured rather than steep.

The last class of new market targets comes in majorly upgraded general market products. Regarding these, management offered:

  • Progress toward gaining design wins significantly improved in the last few quarters.
  • Margins are considerably higher than corporate averages.
  • Long life cycles.
  • Increased engagement with automotive and professional audio customers with new timing products.
  • Sampling high-end analog front-end components.
  • $1B or more SAM.

Higher stock prices are likely measured generally following slower changes.

The summary of visionary growth describes future earnings potential. For example, each billion in new revenue adds $1B times 0.52 (margin) times 0.82 (taxes) divided by 50 million shares equaling approximately $10 per share. This compares to approximately $8 per share for the first $2B in revenue. Cirrus opportunistically targets at least an additional $3.5B in revenue of which it could win a very large percentage as past history suggests at least 50% of the targeted SAM. This is clearly about vision with demonstrated requisite execution.

Risks

Cirrus carries the Apple risk being by far the largest customer generally near 90%. In our view, Cirrus isn't risking losing sockets; it's about Apple performance going forward. On that issue, "Citi analysts expect iPhone sales to rise this year, driven by customers ready to upgrade older devices. The iPhone 17 has proved especially popular, with sales fueling a record year." Both Counterpoint and IDC have been confirming strong sales fueled mainly by a strong iPhone 17 product and a vast group of iPhone users in need of major upgrades. Also, the jury is still out on how far Cirrus can penetrate the PC world. We noticed an interesting misguided comment from Wedbush.

In the semiconductor and PC space, Wedbush sees the “surging computer memory demand” as squeezing companies tied to the traditional PC and handset spaces, negatively impacting Intel Corporation (INTC), HP Inc. (HPQ), Synaptics Incorporated (SYNA), QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM), Qorvo, Inc. (QRVO) and Cirrus Logic.

Really, this makes little sense being that at least for now the PC business is ultra small and going forward markets do change and whatever is there will be additive.

In our view, the biggest risk is for Cirrus to become a story stock, one that trades outside of any rational realm of financial fundamentals. We are selling short calls, thus we stand to lose all the stock should it become so.

Summary & Rating

Our stock rating for a buy came and went during the period in which we wrote this article; thus, we rate Cirrus a hold for now. We expected prices in the mid-$110 - $120. The prices came and went. It may come again.

From a summary standpoint, keys involved with vision investing reside in the following critical parameters.

  • Potential (It's about research).
  • Cash solvency.
  • Human talent.
  • Management integrity.

What isn't important and can be extremely misleading is financial metrics such as DCF or recent revenue growth, even a lack thereof for a few years. Rather, understand what's coming and the timing thereof. Cirrus carries three plus years in product cycle development and implementation. Two-year growth models can be misleading. Investors might wake up one morning and find significant growth seemingly from nowhere. We expect this with Cirrus, by the way.

The growth potential targeted at the bottom line is immense, driven by three very real vectors. Cirrus' past performance suggests that growth could triple earnings in the next three to four years. Of note, we wrote in one of our early articles that a price of $300 plus was possible. Although our timing suggested was way off, the reality of a significantly higher price for the company is more than possible. It is becoming more likely. As a reminder, we are long-term investors. We view investments by decades, not years. Should the price fall back into the $110 - $120 range, we would change the rating to buy once again. But, in our view, Cirrus will most likely significantly outperform the darlings of today going forward. Having help from the eye doctor's tools can be of massive help for investors.

data.title: Investing In The AI Era: Growth, Gains, And Risks
data.description:
By Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

AI investment has not only dominated the equity landscape, but it has also become one of the few forces actively supporting the U.S. economy. In the first half of 2025, technology investment accounted for nearly half of the annual GDP growth, offsetting mounting pressures from trade uncertainty and shifting policy.

While consumer spending remains the primary driver of growth, AI's rapidly expanding role carries significant implications for investors. AI-driven productivity and economic gains could amplify the U.S. stock market rally, extending gains from a handful of dominant AI enablers to a broader group of AI adopters. But that momentum cuts both ways: any pullback in AI investment from major tech firms, or earnings that fail to justify the surge in spending, could quickly heighten economic risks.

After a year fueled by enthusiasm and accelerating innovation, markets may soon shift their attention from AI-powered optimism to the more challenging question of whether growth can be sustained into 2026.

From market phenomenon to macroeconomic force

Despite a cooling economy, the U.S. has shown resilience against various policy shocks this year, and current GDP forecasts predict trend growth in the U.S. economy through 2026 despite ongoing challenges, which has helped underpin equity markets.

Since the Federal Reserve began tightening in spring 2022, the S&P 500 has posted strong returns, buoyed by solid earnings and multiple expansion. The lion’s share of the S&P 500's strength, however, is largely attributed to the “Magnificent 7” tech giants, which have consistently reported strong earnings despite the broader economy facing headwinds. Their performance has provided a safety net for the broader index during times of volatility.

These companies, already leaders before the AI boom, have intensified their investments in AI infrastructure—including data centers, hardware, software, and large model development—to maintain their edge in the AI-driven technology revolution. This investment surge is expected to create new revenue opportunities and enhance profitability.

In essence, what began as a notable equity trend is evolving into a broader economic narrative. With projections indicating that AI-related capital expenditures will reach approximately $1.3 trillion over the next five years, AI is poised to translate capital investments into increased productivity and economic growth across the entire economy.

Divergence in earnings between the Magnificent 7 and the rest of the S&P 500’s constituents

EPS NTM, % appreciation last 3 years

AI’s contribution to economic growth

Though the government does not track AI investments separately, its significant impact on the economy is clear. From data center construction to spending on software and hardware, the recent surge in investment has bolstered the economy amid macroeconomic uncertainties.

AI capital expenditures contributed nearly one percentage point—almost half— to the 2.1% year-on-year growth in GDP during the first half of 2025. This suggests that without the recent wave of tech spending, the economy could have faced a more imminent recession. Even the slowdown in private investment, linked to tariff uncertainties, failed to restrain economic momentum, thanks in large part to AI-related tech investment. More importantly, AI-related investment is not limited to technology firms but has been adopted by companies across a myriad of sectors and industries, thereby acting as a growth driver for the economy as a whole.

It’s worth noting that a large share of the technology fueling this investment—particularly semiconductors and computing hardware—originates abroad, especially from Taiwan and China. This means that the direct contribution of tech spending to measured GDP growth is somewhat diluted once imports are taken into account. But this nuance doesn’t diminish AI’s broader economic influence. When incorporating the multiplier effects of AI—rising equity-market wealth, productivity gains as firms integrate AI into operations, and job creation tied to expanding AI infrastructure—the overall economic impact is likely far greater than what headline GDP data alone suggests.

Tech capex contribution to U.S. real GDP growth

Contributions to y/y % change in real GDP, Q1’23–Q2’25

AI as a buffer, not a replacement

Tech investment has strengthened the economy’s resilience. Still, uncertainty around the ultimate return on AI spending has shifted market attention toward whether growth can be sustained and whether AI will deliver the outcomes investors expect. Against that backdrop, the consumer remains the economy’s most reliable anchor. Despite higher borrowing costs and elevated inflation, households continue to benefit from low unemployment and the wealth accumulated from rising equity and real estate prices.

Consumer spending—roughly 70% of U.S. GDP—expanded by nearly 3% year-over-year in the first half of the year, outpacing the contribution from tech investment and reaffirming its role as the primary engine of economic growth.

AI should therefore be viewed not as a replacement for the consumer, but as a complementary growth driver, one that can bolster the expansion, but cannot sustain it alone.

Investor implications

As tech giants continue to invest heavily in AI infrastructure, the theme is expected to play a larger role in shaping the macroeconomic landscape, and raises two key implications:

  1. Favorable macro environment: If AI yields productivity and economic gains, the U.S. could experience a more favorable macroeconomic climate, expanding the stock market rally beyond a select group of dominant stocks to broader beneficiaries of AI adoption.
  2. Vulnerability to corrections: Conversely, AI’s growing influence also raises concerns about potential corrections. If major tech firms reduce AI investments or fail to meet earnings expectations, or firms in other sectors and industries fail to monetize anticipated AI-related productivity growth, that could amplify downside risks in the markets. What has driven economic growth this year could just as easily contribute to future market downturns.

2025 has been shaped by intense enthusiasm for AI and innovation. The coming year, however, will demand a different lens—one focused on balance-sheet strength, earnings quality, and the durability of growth. With valuations elevated and AI-related debt climbing, the risks of overheating are real. But resilient fundamentals provide a counterweight. Ultimately, the next chapter of the AI era will hinge less on excitement and more on execution: which companies can transform investment into lasting value, and which cannot.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Will Santa Fire Up The S&P Sleigh?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Is Santa ready to fire up the S&P 500 Index (SPX) sleigh? That would fit with the historical pattern this time of year - and the Federal Reserve’s rate decision gives us a fundamental catalyst, too.

Check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here, which comes courtesy of LPL Financial. In their words: "Historically, the index tends to hover around the flatline during the first half of December, with upward momentum building around the 11th trading day."

(Source: LPL Research)

Guess where we are now? Yep... right at “Punch it Rudolph” time.

It doesn’t hurt that the last major potential obstacle to a stock market advance - a monetary policy disappointment - just got pushed aside. Policymakers didn’t vote unanimously in favor of a Fed cut at this week’s meeting. But they delivered a 25-basis point reduction, and that qualifies as “good enough” for the bulls.

Not every stock is participating in this rally (I’m still looking at you, Oracle Corp. (ORCL)!) Key questions about the lackluster job market, lousy consumer sentiment, relatively sticky inflation, and the K-shaped economy will need answering in 2026.

But in the short term, the stage could be a set for a run into year end. So, keep your eyes out for Santa!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Market Fetes Planet Labs' Earnings Beat, But High Multiples, Dilution Risks Loom
data.description:
When last we touched on Planet Labs PBC (PL) in October, the stock had just surged on revenue growth centered around their plan to transition from a data-first company to a satellite maker. I downgraded the stock at the time, from a buy to a hold, because the multiples were starting to look unsustainably high. The price slowly dropped, suggesting momentum was weakening, but then today they issued Q3 earnings, and the price is soaring once again.

It was a modest Q3 beat on both revenue and EPS, but the market acted like this was a real game-changer, with the stock price trending up another 35% in a single day. So naturally, we need to break these new earnings down and figure out if the story has really changed so dramatically or if the momentum is simply getting even further out of hand.

The Q3 Beat: Planet Labs Approaches Break-even Again

For the fiscal year of 2026, Planet Labs has beaten non-GAAP EPS estimates by a few cents, bringing the stock to roughly break-even by that standard. In practice, the GAAP numbers were substantially worse, losing 19¢ per share this quarter, far worse than the 7¢ loss they enjoyed in the same quarter of the prior year. In many regards, this isn’t nearly so exciting an earnings release as the 35% gain in share price would suggest.

Q3 FY2025

Q3 FY2026

Revenue

$61 million

$81 million

Gross Profit

$38 million

$47 million

Operating Income

($23 million)

($18 million)

Net Income

($20 million)

($59 million)

GAAP EPS

(7¢)

(19¢)

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

What we see here is improved revenue, but not much else. Margins are no better, unsurprisingly. Indeed, the conference call noted that the adjusted (non-GAAP) gross margin actually dropped from 64% to 60%.

Revenue growth came almost entirely from the Defense and Intelligence sector, which was up 70% year over year on expanding NATO interest. Civil Government was virtually flat, up just 1%, and the Commercial sector was actually down almost 8%. While this may not bother some investors, I see it as concerning that they’re increasingly dependent on military spending, when priorities on that are likely to change wildly in the future, and NATO might not be content with relying on commercial satellite offerings going forward. It strikes me, to be blunt, as a rather unstable sector, and the fact that it’s up 70% this year suggests to me that it’s a difficult one to project going forward.

While higher revenue allowed Planet Labs to slightly decrease their operating loss, the change in value of their warrants added over $43 million to the net GAAP loss and was a big part of why, from a GAAP perspective, losses got worse this year.

We can see here where this trend continued through the fiscal year so far, despite three consecutive non-GAAP EPS beats.

Q1-Q3 FY2025

Q1-Q3 FY2026

Revenue

$183 million

$221 million

Gross Profit

$102 million

$125 million

Operating Income

($96.8 million)

($59.1 million)

Net Income

($88.0 million)

($94.4 million)

GAAP EPS

(30¢)

(31¢)

Non-GAAP EPS

(13¢)

(3¢)

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

Planet Labs’ growth is shrinking their losses, which is a bright spot, albeit not that big of one, since the revenue isn’t growing so much that we could expect to reach GAAP break-even consistently with stable margins in the near future. Non-GAAP is necessary to massage the numbers to that point, though the positive free cash flow is an encouraging sign of progress.

That’s not to say that Planet Labs has resolved the multiple problem. If anything, Thursday’s share price surge has compounded the issue and potentially created a new near-term risk – dilution.

Multiples and the New Dilution Problem

To start with, we need to look at the balance sheet. In October, the price/book ratio was a very high 10.49, and unsurprisingly, it’s gotten no better.

Cash and Equivalents

$443 million

Total Current Assets

$754 million

Total Current Liabilities

$189 million

Convertible Notes

$446 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$350 million

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

We see a strong current ratio of almost 4.0 and an extremely high price/book ratio of 15.68, which absolutely dwarfs the sector median's already relatively high 3.18. The current assets are high because of a strong cash position, but we can see that it came at a substantial price for Planet Labs, which issued $460 million in convertible notes in September.

That creates an immediate problem, too. Those notes are convertible, and the initial conversion rate is 83.6715 shares per $1,000 of notes, which gives us a rate of around $11.95 per share. The conversion was only meant to be convertible in 2030, or under some very specific circumstances, but one of those circumstances is if they are 30% over the conversion price for 20 trading days in the last 30 trading days of a quarter.

Today’s momentum surge puts Planet Labs stock substantially over that, and barring a large pullback taking it below the $15.50 per share range, there is every reason to expect the notes will be profitably convertible starting next quarter, after January 31.

This isn’t a small amount of stock we’re talking about either. If the full $460 million in notes were converted at this rate, that amounts to adding about 12% to the number of outstanding Class A shares. That’s a lot of dilution and only makes Planet Labs’ already excessive multiples even higher.

Since Planet Labs isn’t GAAP profitable to begin with, they likely needed to take on some debt to continue a period of rather high CapEx. Doing it as a convertible note, however, may in retrospect have been a poor decision, especially when momentum is driving the stock up to unlikely highs.

Beyond dilution, I think the risk is that government spending priorities shift away from Planet Labs’ offerings. I had once envisioned Planet Labs as a company more heavily focused on the commercial sector, but the numbers suggest that right now, it’s the military and intelligence communities driving growth, and their priorities can change on a moment’s notice. A new focus that doesn’t require Planet Labs’ offerings, and suddenly that aspect of growth, the only really sizable one, could look like less of a route to profitability.

It’s possible that Planet Labs’ track for growth continues to work, and work in such a way that the company becomes a consistently profitable venture. Even then, however, I think the market needs to accept multiples that look vastly higher than what the sector generally commands. Whether that will persist remains to be seen.

Pricing Is Getting Out of Hand for Planet Labs’ Numbers

In the end, as much as I hate to do it, I’m going to downgrade Planet Labs from a hold to a sell, entirely on what I believe is momentum disconnected from reality. I just don’t see Planet Labs where it is being worth anywhere near 15 times their underlying book value, and at the current prices, we have to consider substantial dilution from the convertible notes as an inevitability.

I think there’s a good chance Planet Labs does get to the point where they’ll be consistently break-even, or even slightly profitable, but there’s so much positive news priced into the shares already, I just don’t see a fundamental justification to own these shares at this price. If I owned Planet Labs stock, I’d be looking to take some profits. I’m not even saying momentum can’t continue to drive these prices higher, but I don’t see this rise as sustainable. Not by the numbers, anyhow.

Investors who do decide to stick it out should keep an eye on the prices as they relate to the convertible notes. Unless the stock sheds its recent gains in pretty short order, we’re going to see conversion, dilution, and then the risk that lenders will be dumping these shares on the market to take profits. The market hasn’t seemed to appreciate this risk yet, which is why I suggest reconsidering positions before that happens.

data.title: Private Equity Dry Powder Recedes From All-Time Highs Amid Slow Fundraising
data.description:
Private equity and venture capital's substantial dry powder reserves are a little lighter after an extended downturn in fundraising.

Global private equity dry powder — uncommitted capital available for new investments — stood at $2.184 trillion as of March 31. This was down 5.2% from its highest year-end total on record of $2.305 trillion in December 2023, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of the latest available Preqin data.

Venture capital dry powder also hit its year-end high in 2023, peaking at $743.9 billion globally, and was down 19% to $600.9 billion as of March 31.

The declines in dry powder took place amid an extended slump in private equity and venture capital fundraising, with both fundraising totals and fund closes declining globally for three consecutive years between 2022 and 2024. Fundraising was on a turnaround course earlier this year. However, top-tier funds from established managers, who are reaping the benefits of an investor flight-to-quality trend, are having the easiest time securing fresh capital commitments, said Steve Chapman, a partner with accounting firm and private equity consultant Armanino.

"If you're a newer manager trying to raise a $500 million fund, it's going to be tough sledding," Chapman said.

Exits delayed

The relatively slow pace of exits since 2022 remains the primary challenge to private equity and venture capital fundraising, said William Barrett, a managing partner with Reach Capital, a placement agent that connects investors with private funds raising capital.

When portfolio companies are sold or taken public, profits flow back to investors in the form of distributions, who then recycle some of that capital into new fund commitments. But interest rate hikes that eroded corporate valuations and an uncertain macroeconomic outlook are prompting fund managers to hold onto portfolio companies longer.

"That's where most of the stress is, as of today," Barrett said.

A recent uptick in IPOs is reason for "cautious optimism" among venture capital fund managers, said Conor Moore, global head of KPMG's private enterprise division. But it will still take some time and a track record of strong exits to significantly boost fundraising prospects.

"It's probably going to be more going into 2027 after they've shown the exits, as opposed to 2026 itself being a banner year," Moore said.

Private markets growth

Shrinking dry powder reserves are a symptom of a challenging period for private equity and venture capital, but they do not tell the whole story. The institutional investors who supply much of the capital for both asset classes continue to bet big on private markets, and many are pivoting to emerging asset classes such as private credit and private infrastructure, said Gavin Geminder, global head of private equity for KPMG.

Private capital assets under management more than doubled between December 2018 and December 2024, when they exceeded $17.5 trillion globally, according to Market Intelligence's analysis of Preqin data.

Dry powder alone also does not capture all the capital private equity firms have available for investment.

As regulators remove barriers that kept most individual investors out of private markets, funds are preparing for an influx of retail capital that will largely be raised through evergreen fund structures, Geminder said. At the same time, experienced institutional investors continue to prioritize investing directly into private equity deals alongside fund managers via coinvestments, a strategy that limits their exposure to management fees, Geminder added.

Outlook

Geminder forecast that private equity's focus in 2026 would remain squarely on exits and boosting the flow of distributions back to investors. However, it may take time for that to translate into stronger fundraising and a return to expansion mode for dry powder.

"If you're looking for a return to a great fundraising year, '26 is not going to be it," Geminder said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Airbnb: Buy The Dip At Attractive FCF Multiples (Upgrade)
data.description:
It's been a tricky stock market to invest in this year, and 2026 is likely to be no different. While on paper the S&P 500 has soared double digits this year, gains have been concentrated only in a handful of large-cap growth stocks, primarily related to the AI investment trade. Many other previously high-flying stocks, especially those that have exposure to weaker consumer spending, are down.

Airbnb (ABNB) falls in the latter camp. Though the home-sharing platform has recently seen a vast improvement in bookings after faltering earlier this year, the stock remains slightly down since the start of the year. The company has made substantial improvements to its platform that improve its TAM, such as rolling out bookings for services.

I last wrote a neutral article on Airbnb in August, when the stock was trading above $130 per share. Since then, the stock has glided slightly lower. At the same time as bookings are recovering, Airbnb is also beginning to generate a heap of free cash flow that makes its valuation levels look far more attractive. With all of these pieces put together, I'm boosting my rating on Airbnb to a buy.

In my view, these are the core reasons to be long on Airbnb:

  • Airbnb is achieving double-digit bookings growth at scale. In the most recent quarter, Airbnb's ~14% gross bookings growth either matched or outpaced all other peers in the OTA space. The company has tinkered with several pricing and feature changes to encourage better bookings, including transparent pricing for guests and a simplified fee structure for hosts, as well as more lenient cancellation policies.
  • Expansion into services to capture more wallet share. The company launched Services in May, which allows guests to book experiences like an in-home chef and massages. The company is also integrating additional products like travel insurance at checkout. All of these are boosting Airbnb's total bookings potential as guests have a broader menu of services to choose from and pay for.
  • Net repurchaser of shares. Large tech companies are often criticized for excessive amounts of stock-based compensation and share dilution. Airbnb's fully diluted share count, however, has fallen 3% y/y and 5% over a two-year timeframe, reflecting the company's opportunistic buybacks as the stock falls and disciplined compensation.
  • Rich profitability. The company generates ~40% adjusted EBITDA margins and abundant free cash flow to continue funding buybacks and growth initiatives.

We note that after the stock's underperformance this year, Airbnb no longer trades at a substantial premium; in fact, its stock could even be considered "growth at a reasonable price." At current share prices near $128, Airbnb trades at a market cap of $77.52 billion. Netting off the $10.61 billion of cash against $1.99 billion of debt on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $68.90 billion.

For next year, FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Airbnb to hit $13.37 billion in revenue, or 10% y/y growth. If we conservatively assume no further adjusted EBITDA or FCF margin expansion against TTM actual margins of 36% and 38%, adjusted EBITDA in FY26 against this revenue profile would be $4.81 billion, and FCF would be $5.08 billion. This positions Airbnb's valuation multiples at:

  • 14.3x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 13.6x EV/FY26 FCF

We note that Expedia still remains the bona fide value stock in this space (I'm long on Expedia as well), but Airbnb is now sitting at a ~10% discount to its largest rival, Booking Holdings (BKNG).

For a company with incredible growth prospects like Airbnb, I'm content to pay a mid-teens multiple of FCF and adjusted EBITDA. Use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Download

Let's now run through Airbnb's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Airbnb's revenue grew 10% y/y to $4.10 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $4.08 billion (+9% y/y) by a one-point margin.

Of course, all eyes are on Airbnb's bookings rather than its revenue: as a reminder, Airbnb recognizes bookings when customers make reservations and revenue when trips are actually taken. As such, bookings are the best true indicator of near-term customer activity and demand.

In Q3, as shown above, gross bookings value rose 14% y/y to $22.9 billion. This was the second straight quarter of acceleration versus 11% y/y growth in Q2 and 7% y/y growth in Q1. Earlier this year, Airbnb had cautioned that customer demand wasn't waning, but travelers were shortening their booking windows (in other words, booking trips and making reservations closer to the actual dates of travel), driven by more customers waiting for deals and promotions, or perhaps for more economic and job certainty before splurging on a big trip. The sharper jump in bookings in the Q2/Q3 summer season, meanwhile, validates this theory.

But it's not just changing booking behavior that is driving recent growth for Airbnb. The company has also rolled out a number of product initiatives to encourage bookings. Easier and more flexible cancellation policies are core to this strategy, with many properties now offering 14-day free cancellation. The company notes as well that "Reserve Now, Pay Later" offerings on many eligible properties helped drive booking acceleration in Q3.

Competitively, Airbnb's bookings growth in Q3 stacks up well against the same 14% y/y growth pace at Booking (essentially consistent with 13% growth in Q2) and slightly ahead of 12% growth at Expedia (though Expedia more than doubled its growth rate relative to just 5% in Q2).

Rounding out the company's growth initiatives are a greater push to international customers, as well as broadening its available set of service offerings. Per CEO Brian Chesky's remarks on the Q3 earnings call regarding these efforts:

Over the past 12 months, average nights booked in our expansion markets have grown at double the rate of our core markets, and we're also attracting new users to the platform in key markets. For example, first-time bookers were up over 20% in Japan and nearly 50% in India on a year-over-year basis. So this is really encouraging [...]

And we're also scaling supply of high-quality services, experiences. In fact, we received over 110,000 applications from potential hosts. So not only do we think the demands going to be there, the supply is there as well. And that is almost double last quarter to 110,000 applications. And it means people see the value of reaching new customers through Airbnb. But we're not just adding more services, experiences. We know one of the top reasons guests' book experiences because they want to meet other people. So we're making experiences more social."

The company also continues to deliver healthy economies of scale. Adjusted EBITDA grew 5% y/y to $2.05 billion, reflecting a 50% margin, while TTM free cash flow grew 12% y/y over the same period last year to $4.55 billion, or a 38% margin.

Key Takeaways

In my view, investors are still hung up on Airbnb's comments earlier this year on shortening booking windows and potential macro headwinds blurring the outlook for the travel industry. While we certainly do have to worry about an inflation-pinched consumer and the possibility of the macro picture deteriorating next year, we don't see this showing up in Airbnb's bookings yet, and we like the opportunity to invest in this stock at a low-teens FCF multiple. Take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: November $10B+ M&A Deals Set Q4 Up To Outpace Prior Quarter
data.description:
November's announced M&A deals of more than $10 billion in transaction value brought the fourth quarter's worldwide tally to 11 deals.

Through the first two months of the period, the fourth quarter deal count already equaled the third quarter's, but the third quarter's deals were larger in transaction value, with the fourth quarter's total announced transaction value lagging behind the third quarter's total by $87.13 billion, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

The largest deal of the month and the third-largest announcement of the past 12 months, with a transaction value of $48.6 billion, combines two US-based consumer product companies. Dallas, Texas-based Kimberly-Clark Corp. (KMB), maker of products such as Huggies diapers and Kleenex tissues, plans to acquire Summit, New Jersey-based Kenvue Inc. (KVUE).

The acquisition of Kenvue, maker of products such as Tylenol and Band-Aids, will create a consumer goods giant in the US, executives said during a merger call Nov. 3.

The deal announcement came roughly a month after a Trump Administration announcement urging pregnant mothers to avoid taking acetaminophen, citing Kenvue's brand Tylenol and a purported possible link between the drug and autism. The US Food and Drug Administration published a note to physicians on Sept. 22 stating there is not an established causal relationship between acetaminophen and autism.

Barclays analyst Lauren Rae Lieberman referenced the Tylenol headlines during the companies' merger call, asking executives if the noise factored into the deal consideration.

"We reviewed this transaction in the same way that we run the business, with incredible rigor, right, thoughtfulness and discipline," Kimberly-Clark Chairman and CEO Michael Hsu said.

"There is nothing that is more important to us [than] the health of the people who use our products," Kenvue interim CEO Kirk Perry added. "These things have been studied for decades, and we continue to stand by that science as the medical community does as well."

In another of the 20 largest deals announced in the past 12 months, Chicago, Illinois-based Abbott Laboratories (ABT) plans to acquire Madison, Wisconsin-based Exact Sciences Corp. (EXAS) in a deal with a transaction value of $22.54 billion.

Abbott Laboratories is a healthcare and medical devices company, and Exact Sciences has a portfolio of cancer diagnostics technologies, including Cologuard, the leading noninvasive test for colon cancer screening.

The all-cash deal will pay Exact Sciences shareholders $105 per common share, according to the merger announcement.

"This acquisition further strengthens Abbott's leadership position in diagnostics and expands our presence into one of the fastest-growing areas of healthcare," Abbott Laboratories Chairman and CEO Robert Ford said during the company's M&A call Nov. 20.

In a deal announced Nov. 17, New York-based asset manager Clayton Dubilier & Rice LLC plans to acquire Charlotte, North Carolina-based packaging services company Sealed Air Corp. (SEE). The deal has a transaction value of $10.31 billion.

Sealed Air stockholders will receive $42.15 in cash per share, a premium of 24% to Sealed Air's 90-day volume-weighted average price as of Nov. 12, according to the merger announcement. Upon the deal's closing, Sealed Air, which currently trades on the New York Stock Exchange, will become a privately held company, according to the merger announcement.

In a specialty chemicals deal announced Nov. 18 with a transaction value of $12.05 billion, Netherlands-based Akzo Nobel NV (AKZOF) plans to acquire Philadelphia-based Axalta Coating Systems Ltd. (AXTA).

The combination will create a global leader in paints and coatings with $17 billion in revenue and an enterprise value of roughly $25 billion, Akzo Nobel Chairman and CEO Gregoire Poux-Guillaume said during a deal call with analysts.

The all-stock merger of equals provides no premium, Poux-Guillaume said. Axalta shareholders will receive AkzoNobel shares and will own 45% of the combined company, while AkzoNobel shareholders will own 55% of the combined company, Poux-Guillaume said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Recent Pullback Provides Opportunity In Southern Company
data.description:
For many regulated utility investors, The Southern Company (SO) is a stalwart. Founded in 1946, the Atlanta-based provider of both electricity and natural gas was recently named as the top energy company in the US on Newsweek’s World’s Most Trustworthy Companies for 2025. Its stock has likely caused some of its investors to question their faith in the company in recent weeks, however. Since closing near the $100 per share mark in October, SO has lost almost 15% of its value.

I believe that this recent pullback has given investors an opportunity to invest in a dependable, income-producing utility that has multiple avenues for growth in the year ahead. Southern Company is rarely mentioned in the same conversations with other regulated utilities that appear to be set up for increasing demand from data centers and other AI-related themes, but it has several catalysts that could provide a boost for its stock in 2026. I consider the stock to be a Buy at its current price levels for the reasons listed below.

Company Overview

With a market cap of $94.1 billion, The Southern Company is the third-largest US-based company in the utility sector behind only NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE) and Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG). When foreign companies are added to the list, SO is ranked fifth in size. The company has approximately nine million customers with electric utilities in Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi. It controls natural gas utilities in four states in addition to operating wholesale energy operations, fiber optics, and telecommunication services.

The total power-generating capacity for the company is approximately 49 million kilowatts with about half of that capacity coming from natural gas. SO is diversified in its power sources with coal providing 19.0% of its generating capacity and nuclear adding an additional 10.7%. Further detail from the company’s last 10-K regarding the company's power generation is given in the chart below.

The company brought in total revenues of $26.7 billion in FY 2024 and is on pace to collect approximately $29 billion on its top line this year. Retail electric operations account for two-thirds of the company’s total revenues. Natural gas brings in about 16.7%. Wholesale electric business is the third largest revenue source at 9.1%. All other business operations for the company combined account for less than 8%.

SO currently has a dividend yield of 3.46%. Its current quarterly payout is $0.74. All dividends for 2025 have been distributed with the final one sent on December 8. The company has grown its dividend for 24 consecutive years, with the increase traditionally coming in the second quarter of each year.

Analysis of The Southern Company

The Southern Company released its most recent financial results on October 30. Non-GAAP earnings of $1.60 per share topped estimates by 6% or $0.09. This was an 11.9% improvement over the same quarter in 2024. Analysts project that the company will earn $4.28 this year after netting $4.06 per share last year. That year-over-year growth rate is just 5.4%, but SO is expected to earn $4.57 in FY 2026 which would represent an improvement of 6.8%.

These numbers fall in line with the company's stated growth models that include an annual increase in earnings per share of 5% to 7% over the long term. This is supported by regulated electric and gas rate base growth of 8% as well as electric load growth of 8% from 2025 through 2029. There should be little variance from this range as over 90% of the company's earnings are derived from state-regulated utilities.

The Southern Company benefits from a geographic footprint that covers some faster-growing areas of the US. As part of the company's last earnings call, David Poroch, Executive VP and CFO for The Southern Company, stated that economic development across SO's service territories is "robust". Customer electricity usage should continue to increase next year and beyond as 22 companies have made recent announcements to either start or expand their operations in Alabama, Georgia, or Mississippi. These activities, according to Poroch, will generate almost 5000 new jobs as well as capital investments valued at almost $2.8 billion. The Southern Company also reported that residential customer growth is above historical trends in its service area and data center power usage has increased 17% when compared to 2024.

A recent article from Area Development Magazine placed Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi in its top 11 states for business in the US. Georgia was at the top spot in the list. Alabama was eighth. Georgia finished first in two of the magazine's ranking criteria - "favorable regulatory framework" and "logistics and infrastructure". Alabama was first when comparing favorable property tax rates.

The Southern Company has created an aggressive plan for capital improvement through 2029 to capitalize on the opportunities in its service areas. The plan is valued at $76 billion. To reach this goal, SO has stated a need for an additional $9 billion in equity. Prior to its Q2 2025 earnings report, the company priced roughly $2.2 billion of equity through forward sales agreements. In Q3, it recorded an additional $1.8 billion through the same arrangement. The company stated that these contracts contain final settlement dates that extend through the middle of 2027, and SO is entitled to call them earlier than that date. Through these forward sales agreements, additional hybrid security issuances, and SO's internal equity plans, the company has locked up over 77% of its equity need for this CapEx plan.

There is one additional factor working in favor of SO next year. In May, barring an unforeseen turn of events, the Southern Company will officially become a Dividend Aristocrat, having grown its dividend for 25 consecutive years. At that point, it will be eligible to be added to the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index and it will soon after be added to five different linked ETFs. The largest domestic fund of those five is the ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL) which has just over $11 billion in assets under management. This will provide additional demand for SO and could support a higher share price.

Additional Risks to Consider

There are a few risks to consider before investing in The Southern Company. The first and perhaps most concerning for many investors is the company’s debt load. As recently as August of this year, Fitch affirmed the company’s debt rating at one of the lower levels of the investment grade scale, BBB+. The bond rating agency noted the company’s regulated business profile and conservative management of its non-regulated subsidiary as positive for the company. However, SO's last income statement showed the company had made $2.3 billion in interest payments in the first nine months of this year. That represented a 14.3% increase from the same period in 2024.

Like almost all regulated utility stocks, SO is sensitive to changes in interest rates and could be impacted negatively by an increase in long-term rates. Utilities are common investment targets for income-focused investors. Their dividends compete with the yield from fixed income options like bond funds and certificates of deposit. When their interest rates rise, the dividends from utility stocks become less attractive as stocks always involve added risks.

Dependence on nuclear power sources always brings added risks related to their safety and regulation. They are expensive to build and bring added environmental concerns. The current federal regulatory environment is favorable for nuclear energy, but that could change in the future. The Southern Company has positioned itself as a leader in carbon-free power generation. Southern Nuclear, a subsidiary of The Southern Company, operates the Vogtle Electric Generating Plant in Waynesboro, Georgia. The nuclear facility has opened two new units in the last two years, which made them the first new units built in the US in the last three decades.

The Bottom Line

When SO was flirting with the $100 price level, it may have gotten ahead of itself in valuation, but now that it has taken a significant step back, I think the stock deserves more attention. The company provides investors with stable revenues from its regulated businesses, a strong track record of dividends that should give its stock some added buying interest later in 2026, and a pipeline for growth through its capital investments in the years ahead. The company’s debt load is certainly a concern as it must manage a high level of interest payments, but it has been resourceful in finding various ways to raise capital to maintain its investment-grade status.

There have been a few Wall Street analysts who have downgraded their opinion of the company in recent months. Jeffries moved SO from a Buy to a Hold last month, but its price target is still north of $100 per share. Goldman Sachs issued a similar downgrade one day later, moving its price target to $98. Both of these actions are related to election results from the Georgia Public Service Commission that have given cause for added concern that future rate increases may be harder to pass. However, back in July of this year, the commission had already instituted a rate freeze for Georgia Power through the end of 2028, and Republicans still hold a majority on the commission with three out of five seats.

I consider SO to be a Buy heading into the new year. As investors reassess the landscape for utility companies, a more stable option like Southern Company may have added favor over others who have been riding the greater AI hype. I think the potential rewards for owning the stock outweigh the risks referenced here.

data.title: U.S. Coal Prices Push Higher With Support From Natural Gas
data.description:
US coal market prices extended a steady rally in November, with export and domestic benchmarks making gains.

Through 2027, firm natural gas prices are expected to support increased coal generation, driving stable or growing coal production. After 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity, incentivized in part by broad-based tax credits. Overall, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, with much of the planned retirement activity deferred until after 2030.

Coal plant generation share through 2035 is forecast to decline slowly to just 7.5% from 18.7% of total generation in 2026. The rapid phaseout of tax credits under the recently approved US tax and spending bill — also referred to as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act — is forecast to slow the wind and solar generation growth, allowing coal plants to run more often after 2030. The most production uplift will take place at the Powder River Basin (PRB), followed by the Illinois Basin (ILB), as the reduced growth of wind and solar generation mainly affects the generation mix in the Midcontinental US.

The take

  • US coal prices again moved higher in November, with production and shipments essentially flat year over year for the month.
  • The S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast anticipates 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, amid market pressure to retain existing generation due to greater reliability needs and slower forecast growth in solar and wind energy.
  • The production outlook varies by region, with the Powder River Basin and Illinois Basin expecting stable to slightly growing production through 2027, while Appalachian coal production is forecast to decline due to reduced domestic demand and limited export growth.

Coal prices gained across the board in November, impacting export and domestic benchmarks. CAPP region export benchmarks added $1.00/short ton to $81.00/short ton (1.3%), NYMEX CAPP added $2.00/short ton (2.6%) to $78.00/short ton, and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 Btu per pound gained $2.00/short ton (3.3%) to $62.00/short ton. Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur moved higher by $1.00/short ton to $51.25/short ton, while the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark gained $0.10/short ton to $15.00/short ton.

Winter weather settled across most of the continental US during November, supporting growth in natural gas prices. Henry Hub spot gas opened at $3.57/MMBtu and eased to a mid-month low of $3.49/MMBtu before rallying to close November at $4.12/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $3.73/MMBtu for the month. Storage withdrawals began with the arrival of heating demand, with working gas reaching its likely maximum level of 3,946 BCF as of Nov. 14 — 146 BCF above the five-year average and 23 BCF above the same week of 2024.

Regional gas market prices were correlated, with late summer discounts to Henry Hub holding steady. Chicago Gate averaged $3.40/MMBtu, a $0.33/MMBtu discount to Henry Hub. TCO Pool's discount shrank to $0.50/MMBtu for a monthly average of $3.23/MMBtu, while TETCO M3's discount shrank to $0.44/MMBtu for a monthly average of $3.29/MMBtu. SoCal Border averaged $2.97/MMBtu in November, $0.76/MMBtu below Henry Hub.

The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) estimated August 2025 coal stockpiles at 105 million short tons (MMst), a modest 4 MMst decrease from July, indicating generally mild summer conditions.

Current forward pricing for PRB coal has been flat and stable, reflecting sufficient inventories at power plants and mining capacity to increase production as needed against firmer natural gas prices. After 2027, lower natural gas prices and declining coal demand are forecast to restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal price levels are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's price levels making domestic coal generation generally less competitive against Northeast natural gas. Firmer eastern natural gas prices have nevertheless boosted demand for the first half of 2025, even as seaborne coal demand is expected to decline 7.7% year-over-year due to reduced demand from India and China.

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $65/short ton suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal, with Atlantic Basin export above that threshold and coal competing in Pacific Basin export markets generally below. After declining in 2024, bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and more supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and the overall Eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 71 MMst in 2025–30.

Outlook for US coal production, demand

For the four weeks ending Nov. 15, coal shipments averaged 9.9 MMst, 2.0% above 2024 levels. After a relatively mild summer, somewhat elevated inventories have brought shipments back into line with 2024.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027. Gas-to-coal switching during the first quarter of 2025 reduced coal inventory surpluses from 2024-end, setting up production growth in 2025. We now forecast coal production at 536 MMst, an increase of 24 MMst (4.7%) from 2024 levels. Coal generation is forecast to further gain market share from natural gas through 2027, until relative coal and gas pricing normalizes and expanding green energy again puts pressure on coal generation. The overall coal market, including domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline 141 MMst between 2025 and 2030.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

Production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) for the second quarter of 2025 indicate the year's first-half production at 110.4 MMst, or an annualized rate of 220.9 MMst. Production is now forecast at 218 MMst in 2025, with higher inventories potentially constraining production in the second half. Production is forecast for modest growth through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, S&P Global Energy projects that coal retirements in the Midwest and expansion of wind generation in PRB's core markets will gradually shrink the coal demand to 192 MMst, declining further to 156 MMst through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

MSHA's 2025 June quarter production reports indicate first-half production at 34.7 MMst, or an annualized rate of 69.5 MMst. We forecast stable annual production of 78 MMst through 2027, after which the expansion of wind generation and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode the ILB coal demand. Coal production in the ILB is forecast to fall to 60 MMst by 2030, declining further to 39 MMst by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

MSHA's production reports for the June quarter of 2025 indicate first-half production at 80.2 MMst, or an annualized rate of 160.4 MMst. Appalachian coal demand tends to be more sensitive to global seaborne markets than to domestic natural gas prices, compared to the PRB or the ILB — which are forecast for improved demand against natural gas generation — therefore, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast production at 165 MMst, 5.1% higher than 2024. As remaining domestic demand erodes after 2027, with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 112 MMst by 2030.

Further information

Market indicative coal forecasts by S&P Global Energy represent forward curves for spot-traded instruments, analogous to a strip of contracts. The shorter tenors — current year and prompt year, plus additional years, if available — are driven by the observed/assessed market. The longer tenors — typically forecast years three to 20 for physically assessed markers — are driven by fundamental estimates of cash costs of production, accepted returns to capital, regional productive capacity, and forecast supply and demand. For the long-tenured portion of the curve, S&P Global Energy forecasts prices for specific coal markers and defines the remaining markers via historical spreads.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Janus Henderson Short Duration Flexible Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Greg Wilensky, CFA and Michael Keough

Investment environment

  • The U.S. short-term fixed income market registered a positive return for the quarter. Short-term Treasury yields rallied, helping to drive positive performance. Spread sectors outperformed comparable maturity U.S. Treasuries.
  • In September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates for the first time since December 2024, in response to signs of a slowdown in labor demand. In August, non-farm payroll growth was below expectations, with just 22,000 jobs created, together with downward revisions to prior months’ numbers.
  • While policymakers indicated they anticipate two additional cuts in 2025 and one more in 2026, markets are pricing in more aggressive cuts in 2026. Given that inflation likely will remain above target levels, the market’s expectation for rate cuts may be called into question, unless the labor market continues to weaken.
  • The U.S. two-year Treasury yield ended the quarter 11 basis points (bps) lower at 3.61%. Investment-grade corporate credit spreads tightened nine bps to 74 bps, while high-yield spreads narrowed 23 bps to 267 bps, as investor sentiment remained upbeat.

Portfolio review

We strive to generate moderate income from credit spread sectors while limiting drawdowns. We seek to do this by constructing the portfolio with credit risk and a modest amount of interest rate risk over the cycle. Our overweight allocation to credit-spread risk was the primary contributor to relative performance, as spreads tightened through the quarter on robust corporate profitability, expected Fed easing, and economic growth supported by artificial Intelligence (AI)-driven investments and tax cuts.

Our positive view on credit-spread risk reflects solid corporate and consumer fundamentals and continued technical strength across fixed income, despite spreads (especially corporate spreads) trading at historically tight levels. Securitized spreads continue to look attractive relative to corporates.

Specific contributors included allocations to high-yield corporates and agency mortgage-backed securities, as well as an underweight allocation to government-related securities. Exposure to asset-backed securities detracted. At the sector level, security selection in healthcare and supermarkets also detracted.

We increased our exposure to corporate debt, given the stable economic outlook, as well as to collateralized loan obligations, where valuations continue to look attractive on an absolute and relative basis. To help fund these purchases, we reduced our exposure to agency mortgage-backed securities following their strong performance during the quarter.

Our duration and yield curve positioning performed in line with the benchmark over the quarter. We remained overweight duration primarily as a hedge for our large allocation to high-quality credit spread sectors. We believe shorter-term Treasury rates would fall if the economy deteriorated more than expected.

Manager outlook

Tariff impacts have been relatively muted so far but are beginning to push some prices higher. While gross domestic product (GDP) growth is likely to slow in the second half of the year, third-quarter GDP growth estimates have recently started moving up toward 3%, and we believe future economic growth will benefit from both rates and tax cuts.

We prefer a small duration overweight focused on the front end of the yield curve. Given the slowdown in job creation, we expect the Fed to continue to cut rates. Current policy is modestly restrictive by the Fed’s own assessment, yet the timing of further moves likely will depend on incoming data. The Fed is watching for potential inflation persistence from tariffs, and would, in our view, only aggressively cut rates below neutral if the labor market significantly deteriorates.

Since Liberation Day, credit spreads have continued their march tighter, with spreads in many asset classes now looking rich on a historical basis. While we now see less scope for broad-based spread compression within fixed income, the Fed’s easing increases our confidence that we are in an environment that favors a carry, or income, approach.

Volatility in the first half of the year allowed us to capitalize on asset allocation opportunities provided by market dislocations. It also highlighted the importance of remaining diversified as rate-spread correlations come down. With spreads hovering around their cyclical tight levels, we are focused on maximizing carry-per-unit of risk while also seeking idiosyncratic opportunities to add value. Amid a backdrop of elevated macro uncertainty, nimble asset allocation and careful security selection can be reliable levers to generate performance. Attractive valuations and strong credit quality within securitized sectors support our overweight to securitized credit and tie into our theme of maximizing carry and managing downside risk.

Many fixed income sectors continue to offer attractive nominal yields in the mid-to-high single digits, while bonds have once again begun to exhibit their diversification benefits by way of low or negative correlation to equities and negative rate-spread correlation. Taken together, we consider the combination of diversification benefits and attractive nominal yields essential to managing portfolios through the evolving economic landscape.

Definitions

Basis point (bp) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Duration measures a bond price’s sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond’s duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

Quantitative Easing (QE) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market. Carry is the excess income earned from holding a higher yielding security relative to another.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

2-year Treasury yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature two years from the date of purchase. Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

Diversification neither assures a profit nor eliminates the risk of experiencing investment losses.

Monetary policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money. Correlation measures the degree to which two variables move in relation to each other. A value of 1.0 implies movement in parallel, -1.0 implies movement in opposite directions, and 0.0 implies no relationship.

The opinions are as of 09/30/25, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Mutual fund performance for some periods may incorporate returns from older share classes, adjusted for fees and expenses. Visit janushenderson.com/synthetic for specific details. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

Performance attribution is a method used to analyze and quantify the performance of an investment portfolio relative to its benchmark and does not represent actual performance. Attribution is calculated using gross returns before the deduction of advisory fees and considers differences in weighting for the portfolio and the index. This analysis aims to break down the portfolio's performance into different components to understand what factors contributed to relative return, among other performance characteristics. The analysis can provide insights into which decisions positively or negatively impacted the portfolio's performance and serves as the basis for this discussion.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

High-yield or "junk" bonds involve a greater risk of default and price volatility and can experience sudden and sharp price swings.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

ESG Integration Risk. There is a risk that considering ESG Factors as part of the Fund’s investment process may fail to produce the intended results or that the Fund may perform differently from funds that have a similar investment style but do not formally incorporate such considerations in their strategy. Information related to ESG Factors provided by issuers and third parties, which portfolio management may utilize, continues to develop, and may be incomplete or inaccurate, use different methodologies, or be applied differently across issuers and industries.

ESG Integration. As part of its investment process, portfolio management considers ESG risks and opportunities (“ESG Factors”) that it believes are financially material, alongside other fundamental investment factors. Examples of potential financially material ESG Factors include corporate governance, company culture, exposure to climate change, and human capital management. To assess ESG Factors, portfolio management uses issuer reports, third-party data, and internally-generated analyses and may engage directly with issuers. ESG Factors are one of many considerations in the investment decision-making process and may not be determinative in deciding to include or exclude an investment from the portfolio.

Bloomberg 1-3 Year U.S. Government/Credit Index measures Treasuries, government-related issues and corporates with maturity between 1-3 years. Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.
